sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 55, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 54 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The squares moved as expected:
- LEFT: moved from cols 24/34 to cols 19/39 (apart by 5)
- LEFT again: moved from cols 19/39 to cols 14/44 (apart by 5)
- DOWN: moved from rows 44 to 49
- SPACE: no board change, no level completion
Now the squares are at rows 49, cols 14/44. The initial position was rows 49, cols 19/39. So the squares can go further left than the initial position.
I need to understand the game better. Let me think about what I know:
1. Two 5x5 squares move in a container
2. They move in steps of 5 pixels
3. LEFT/RIGHT move them apart/together
4. UP/DOWN move them up/down
5. SPACE doesn't seem to do anything meaningful to the game board
6. The container has a complex shape
Wait - maybe I should look at this from a different angle. The container boundary starts at [[9, 19], [9, 48], [13, 48]]. The container spans from row 9 to some lower row, and from col 19 to col 48. The squares are inside this container.
Let me think about what the game might be. Looking at the image, there are two chambers (yellow left, orange right) separated by black walls. The squares are in these chambers. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Move the squares to specific positions that match some pattern
2. Or move the squares until they're in the right configuration
Let me look at the container structure more carefully. The boundary [[9, 19], [9, 48], [13, 48]] suggests the container starts at row 9, with the top spanning from col 19 to 48. Let me see the full boundary to understand the shape.
Actually, I should focus on finding the goal. Let me check if there are any visual cues in the container that indicate target positions. Maybe there are colored markers or the squares need to be at specific positions.
Let me try a different approach: systematically explore all possible positions and check if any position triggers level completion. The squares can move in 5-pixel steps. Let me figure out the bounds:
- Vertical: from row 9 (container top) to some lower bound
- Horizontal: left square from some min col to max col, right square from min col to max col
Let me try moving the squares to the top of the container and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Squares at rows 49, cols 14/44. Let me explore the container bounds.
# Container top is at row 9. Let me try moving squares to the top.

# First, let me see the full container boundary
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 1325:
        print("Container boundary:", n['boundary'])
        break

# Try moving UP to see how high squares can go
# Current row: 49. Container top: row 9. 
# Let me try multiple UP moves
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter 4x UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check if we can go higher
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter 5th UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Only affects HUD bars", "LEFT": "Moves squares apart horizontally by 5 cols", "RIGHT": "Moves squares together horizontally by 5 cols", "UP": "Moves squares up by 5 rows", "DOWN": "Moves squares down by 5 rows"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black_containers": "Container with two chambers (1325 pixels)", "hud_bars": "Top/bottom timer bars"}, "hud_map": {"top_border": "Timer bar", "bottom_border": "Timer bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Two 5x5 sky blue squares in black container (1325 px). Container top at row 9, cols 19-48. Squares move in 5-pixel steps. Current: rows 49, cols 14/44.", "goal_model": "Unknown - exploring position space.", "recent_findings": ["Squares can go to cols 14/44 (leftmost tested)", "RIGHT blocked at cols 24/34", "SPACE has no game effect"], "open_questions": ["What triggers level completion?", "What are the movement bounds?"], "current_plan": "Explore vertical bounds, then test all positions for level completion.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE brute force", "SPACE at various positions"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
